detail.headline_scope: Choosing your domain + writing a 3-sentence problem statement
detail.notes: All content stays within the headline boundary. Section 5.4 ('What Makes a Problem Statement Good?') tests quality of the statement being written — directly in scope. No specification writing, requirements engineering, user stories, or full project planning is taught. The single mention of 'Full specification writing' in §7 is a one-sentence named-and-deferred pointer.

'problem domain' defined on first use in §5.1; 'problem statement' defined on first use in §5.2; '3-sentence format' defined in §5.3. No acronyms used without spell-out. No paragraph stacks 3+ new terms. Sentences are short, direct, and active. No hedging language detected. 'Specification Portfolio' appears only as a forward assessment reference, not as a defined technical concept.

verdict: PASS — all 6 checks pass. Corpus is within atomic word-count range (~1780 words). Primary resource [1] is cited throughout. Sections 3, 4, 5, and 10 meet minimum word thresholds. No forward references or sequence drift detected; specification writing is correctly named-and-deferred. No scope creep: the corpus stays strictly within 'choosing a domain + writing a 3-sentence problem statement'. Beginner readability is strong with all new terms defined on first use and no jargon dumps.
